heroOverview: Macrogenia is an over-development or excessive forward projection of the chin caused by an enlarged mandibular symphysis or anterior mandibular body, producing a chin that appears disproportionately large relative to the rest of the face. The condition is classified under dentofacial anomalies and malocclusion in ICD-10 (M26.05) and is evaluated by oral and maxillofacial surgeons, orthodontists, and craniofacial specialists. It occurs in isolation or as part of broader skeletal Class III jaw relationships where the lower jaw projects forward relative to the upper. Prevalence data specific to macrogenia are limited, but dentofacial anomalies as a group affect approximately 20 to 30 percent of the population to some degree; clinically significant macrogenia requiring surgical correction is uncommon. For most patients, the primary concern is aesthetic; however, prominent chins can co-occur with occlusal disharmony, temporomandibular joint dysfunction, or obstructive sleep apnea, making thorough functional assessment important before treatment planning.

keyStats.prevalence: Precise isolated macrogenia prevalence is unreported; dentofacial anomalies affect approximately 20-30% of the population broadly; clinically significant macrogenia requiring surgery is uncommon
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females; aesthetic concern and surgical candidacy peak in adulthood after skeletal maturity (women ~16-17 years, men ~18-19 years)
keyStats.avgAge: Surgery typically performed after skeletal maturity: late teens to early adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Global epidemiological data specific to macrogenia not available; orthognathic surgery is performed in approximately 40-50 per 100,000 population annually in high-income countries

definition: Macrogenia (ICD-10: M26.05) refers to abnormal enlargement or excessive anterior projection of the chin as defined by the dimensional excess of the mandibular symphysis or the anterior mandibular body. Clinically, the chin appears disproportionately large in relation to other facial structures. The condition must be distinguished from prognathism — forward projection of the entire mandible relative to the maxilla — although the two frequently co-exist. The excess may be primarily vertical (a long, vertically prominent chin), primarily horizontal (a chin that projects forward beyond the normal sagittal limit), or combined. Macrogenia falls within the specialty of oral and maxillofacial surgery and craniofacial orthodontics. Diagnosis is made using standardised lateral cephalometry and three-dimensional facial imaging, with reference to population norms for chin projection and vertical facial height. When the chin enlargement is part of a broader skeletal Class III malocclusion, comprehensive orthognathic surgical planning involving both the upper and lower jaws is required. Isolated macrogenia without occlusal disorder is amenable to genioplasty alone. Co-occurring conditions include Class III malocclusion, temporomandibular joint dysfunction, and in extreme skeletal cases, obstructive sleep apnea from altered upper airway anatomy.

typesClassification.description: Excessive anterior projection of the chin in the sagittal plane, causing the chin point to protrude beyond the normal facial profile; may occur with or without overall mandibular prognathism.
Excessive inferior height of the chin, producing a long lower facial third; often associated with open-bite tendencies and a convex lower lip.
Enlargement in both sagittal and vertical dimensions, producing the most pronounced chin deformity; typically requires genioplasty with both posterior setback and vertical reduction.

typesClassification.type: Macrogenia as part of skeletal Class III relationship
typesClassification.description: The enlarged chin is part of a broader prognathic mandible — the entire lower jaw protrudes relative to the upper — requiring orthognathic surgery beyond isolated genioplasty.

primarySymptoms: A chin that appears disproportionately large, long, or protruding relative to the nose, midface, and forehead when viewed in profile or from the front.
Facial asymmetry if the chin excess is more pronounced on one side, visible on frontal view.
Difficulty achieving lip closure at rest, particularly in vertical macrogenia where the chin height elongates the lower facial third and stretches the lips apart.
Dental malocclusion — typically Class III (lower teeth sitting in front of upper teeth) — if the chin enlargement is part of a prognathic mandibular relationship.
Temporomandibular joint discomfort or clicking sounds in patients where the jaw relationship creates uneven load distribution across the joint.
Soft tissue chin pad prominence that amplifies the skeletal projection when viewed externally.
Self-consciousness or psychological distress from perceived facial imbalance, particularly when the deformity affects professional or social confidence.
Lower lip eversion or fullness when the chin projects forward, displacing the lower lip anteriorly relative to the upper.
Speech articulation difficulties in extreme cases where the jaw and chin relationship significantly disrupts the dental and lip relationships needed for certain phonemes.
Neck and posture compensation — some patients adopt a slightly tilted head position to minimise the visual appearance of a prominent chin.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive chin prominence during adolescent growth that outpaces the development of the midface and upper jaw.
Increasingly noticeable lower facial length creating imbalance in profile photographs or mirrors.
New or worsening lip incompetence — difficulty keeping the mouth comfortably closed — as the chin height increases.
Parental or patient concern during orthodontic review that the chin and lower jaw are not tracking normally with facial growth.
emergencySigns: Rapidly progressive chin enlargement in an adult, raising concern for acromegaly (growth hormone excess) or an underlying mandibular tumour requiring urgent investigation.
Severe temporomandibular joint pain, locking, or acute inability to open the mouth in the context of a known jaw relationship disorder.
Obstructive sleep apnea presenting with witnessed apneas, extreme daytime sleepiness, and oxygen desaturations, particularly in patients with co-existing skeletal anomalies affecting the airway.
Signs of nerve compression — numbness, tingling, or pain in the lower lip and chin — suggesting pathological involvement of the inferior alveolar or mental nerve.

causes.cause: Skeletal overgrowth of the mandibular symphysis
causes.description: Excess proliferation of the bony chin during craniofacial development produces dimensional excess in either the horizontal or vertical plane; the precise regulatory mechanisms are incompletely understood but involve genetic programming of mandibular growth centres.

Mandibular morphology has a strong heritable component; macrogenia and skeletal Class III relationships cluster in families, with twin studies estimating heritability of chin projection at 60 to 80 percent.

Growth hormone excess from a pituitary adenoma stimulates continued periosteal bone deposition across the mandible, including the symphysis, producing progressive macrogenia and prognathism in adults; this is an acquired medical cause requiring endocrine evaluation.

Abnormal bone remodelling in Paget's disease can affect the mandible, producing gradual expansion of the chin and lower jaw; onset is typically in middle age or later and is associated with elevated alkaline phosphatase.

Unilateral or bilateral condylar overgrowth displaces the lower jaw downward and forward, secondarily increasing the apparent chin prominence by moving the entire mandible into a more protruded position.

The normal aesthetic range for chin projection varies substantially across populations; what is classified as macrogenia by Caucasian cephalometric norms may represent normal facial anatomy in other ethnic groups, highlighting the importance of population-appropriate reference values.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of skeletal Class III jaw relationship or prominent chin
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives with mandibular prognathism or macrogenia substantially increase the likelihood of a similar pattern in offspring, reflecting the high heritability of mandibular morphology.

Males typically have greater mandibular dimensions than females, and the mandible continues growing longer in males — until around 18-19 years — providing more time for a growth excess to accumulate and be expressed clinically.

Active acromegaly from a pituitary adenoma is the most important acquired cause of progressive macrogenia in adults; the condition is treatable but requires early recognition to limit skeletal change.

riskFactors.factor: Paget's disease of bone affecting the mandible
riskFactors.description: Mandibular involvement in Paget's disease leads to progressive enlargement; incidence of Paget's disease increases with age, making it relevant in older adults presenting with progressive chin change.
Overgrowth of one or both condyles drives the mandible forward and downward, secondarily increasing apparent chin projection and often requiring surgical management of both the condyle and chin.

riskFactors.factor: Age at assessment relative to skeletal maturity
riskFactors.description: Assessing chin prominence before skeletal maturity risks underestimating the final deformity; treatment planning should be deferred until growth is complete to avoid relapse after surgical correction.

affectedDemographics: Adults of any ethnicity presenting after skeletal maturity with a disproportionately prominent chin
Adolescents in whom progressive chin growth is identified during orthodontic monitoring
Adults with acromegaly, in whom chin prominence is an endocrine sign rather than a primary jaw disorder
Older adults with Paget's disease of bone affecting the mandible
Individuals in populations where Class III jaw relationships are more prevalent, including some East Asian populations
Patients seeking facial aesthetic surgery or orthognathic surgery who identify the chin as the primary concern
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of macrogenia is made by a combination of clinical facial examination, standardised lateral cephalometric radiography, and three-dimensional imaging such as cone-beam CT. The clinical assessment documents facial proportions using the rule of thirds — dividing the face into equal upper, middle, and lower thirds — and assesses chin projection relative to the nose and lower lip using established soft-tissue profile norms. Lateral cephalometric analysis measures the position of the bony chin point (Pogonion) relative to established reference planes (Frankfort horizontal, Nasion-Pogonion line) and to normative data. A skeletal Class III relationship is identified when the mandible protrudes beyond the maxilla using standard angular and linear measurements. Dental occlusion is assessed clinically and on articulated dental models. Cone-beam CT provides three-dimensional volumetric analysis of the chin and mandible, which is particularly useful in asymmetric cases. Temporomandibular joint assessment — clinical examination, joint sounds, mandibular range of motion — is performed routinely. If acromegaly is suspected from progressive change in an adult, serum insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) and oral glucose tolerance test with GH measurement are ordered. Polysomnography is arranged if obstructive sleep apnea is a co-existing concern. Orthodontic evaluation assesses dental compensation and occlusal goals before surgical planning begins.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures skeletal and dental relationships to quantify the chin excess, identify the underlying jaw relationship, and provide the dimensional data needed for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standardised X-ray of the skull in a side-on profile taken with the teeth in occlusion; you stand in a frame that positions the head precisely; total radiation is low, similar to a panoramic dental X-ray.

diagnosticTests.test: Cone-beam CT (CBCT) of the mandible and midface
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides three-dimensional bony anatomy for precise surgical planning, particularly for asymmetric cases and for virtual surgical simulation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You sit or stand in a CBCT unit while a rotating arm scans the head; the scan takes 10-20 seconds; radiation dose is moderate, significantly less than a medical CT.

diagnosticTests.test: Dental models and digital occlusal analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the current dental occlusion and allows the orthodontist and surgeon to plan tooth movements needed to achieve a functional bite after surgical correction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An impression or digital intraoral scan is taken of both upper and lower teeth; this is non-invasive and typically completed at an orthodontic appointment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents facial proportions, profile, and symmetry for surgical planning, treatment outcome comparison, and for virtual two-dimensional simulation of anticipated results.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Photographs are taken in a clinic setting with standardised positioning — frontal, lateral, and 45-degree oblique views; no pain or radiation involved.

diagnosticTests.test: IGF-1 and GH suppression test (if acromegaly suspected)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for active growth hormone excess, which would be the primary treatment target before any surgical correction of the chin.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample measures IGF-1; if elevated, an oral glucose tolerance test measures how well GH suppresses after glucose ingestion; involves a blood draw before and at 30-minute intervals over 2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses obstructive sleep apnea when symptoms such as snoring, witnessed apneas, or daytime sleepiness are present, since jaw relationship surgery may affect upper airway calibre.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An overnight sleep study with sensors monitoring brain activity, breathing, oxygen levels, and movement; performed in a sleep centre or with a home device, depending on the clinical situation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of macrogenia is primarily surgical when the deformity is clinically significant and causes functional or aesthetic impairment that has failed to resolve with observation or orthodontic management. Genioplasty — surgical repositioning of the chin bone — is the definitive procedure for isolated macrogenia. In a sliding genioplasty, the surgeon makes an osteotomy (bone cut) below the tooth roots through the chin, then moves the freed chin segment posteriorly (to reduce forward projection), superiorly (to reduce vertical height), or both, fixing it in the new position with titanium mini-plates and screws. When macrogenia is part of a broader skeletal Class III malocclusion, genioplasty is combined with bimaxillary orthognathic surgery — repositioning the upper jaw (Le Fort I osteotomy) and lower jaw (bilateral sagittal split osteotomy) to correct the overall jaw relationship before the chin is addressed. Orthodontic treatment before and after surgery aligns the teeth within the corrected skeletal framework, typically spanning twelve to twenty-four months total. Soft tissue chin reduction — burring or removal of the soft tissue chin pad — can reduce soft tissue prominence without skeletal surgery in patients with excess soft tissue rather than bony excess. For patients with excess caused by active acromegaly or Paget's disease, medical or endocrine treatment of the underlying condition takes priority before any surgical planning. Non-surgical approaches including augmentative orthodontics and camouflage therapy have limited impact on pronounced skeletal macrogenia but may be appropriate for milder presentations in patients who decline surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Medical management of acromegaly (prior to surgical correction)
medicalTreatments.description: Somatostatin analogues (octreotide, lanreotide) or GH receptor antagonists (pegvisomant) suppress growth hormone activity in acromegaly, arresting further chin and mandibular growth. Pituitary surgery or radiotherapy addresses the adenoma. Correction of acromegaly must precede any elective jaw surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Biochemical control of acromegaly achieved in 50-70% with somatostatin analogues alone; combined with pituitary surgery, remission rates exceed 80% in experienced centres.

medicalTreatments.name: Bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy for Paget's disease
medicalTreatments.description: When Paget's disease involves the mandible, bisphosphonates suppress osteoclast-driven bone turnover, reducing progressive enlargement and bone pain before surgical revision is considered.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Alkaline phosphatase normalisation in 70-80% of treated patients; bisphosphonate therapy significantly slows disease progression in Paget's disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Pre-surgical orthodontics
medicalTreatments.description: Orthodontic treatment aligns the teeth and removes dental compensations that developed to accommodate the jaw discrepancy, ensuring the bite will fit correctly after surgical repositioning. This phase typically runs 12-18 months before surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Essential component of orthognathic surgery success; combined orthodontic-surgical treatment achieves stable, functional occlusion in over 90% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Post-surgical orthodontics and retention
medicalTreatments.description: After orthognathic surgery, orthodontic finishing refines the occlusion and retention appliances or retainers maintain the surgical result while bone healing consolidates. Post-surgical orthodontic treatment usually runs 6-12 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Post-surgical orthodontics achieves detailed occlusal refinement that surgery alone cannot provide; retention reduces relapse risk from under 15% to below 5% in well-planned cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Sliding genioplasty
surgicalOptions.description: An osteotomy is made below the tooth roots through the mental symphysis, the chin bone is mobilised, repositioned to reduce horizontal projection or vertical height (or both), and fixed with titanium mini-plates. The gold-standard procedure for isolated macrogenia, it allows three-dimensional repositioning.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction rates of 85-95% for aesthetic outcome; relapse of less than 3mm in over 90% of cases at 2-year follow-up in series using rigid fixation.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral sagittal split osteotomy (BSSO) with or without genioplasty
surgicalOptions.description: When macrogenia is part of a Class III skeletal malocclusion, BSSO sets back the lower jaw and is combined with genioplasty if residual chin prominence remains. It is the most common orthognathic procedure for mandibular prognathism.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Greater than 90% long-term skeletal stability when combined with pre-surgical orthodontics and rigid internal fixation; relapse requiring revision in under 5% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Soft tissue chin reduction (mentoplasty)
surgicalOptions.description: For patients in whom soft tissue excess rather than bony prominence is the dominant feature, direct excision or liposuction of the chin pad reduces apparent size without skeletal osteotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: High patient satisfaction for appropriately selected cases; less applicable when the deformity is primarily skeletal.

alternativeTreatments.name: Orthodontic camouflage
alternativeTreatments.description: Dental extractions and orthodontic movement can produce modest apparent reduction in chin projection by retracting the lower incisors and reducing lower lip prominence, without addressing the underlying bony excess; appropriate only for mild cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Digital simulation and watch-and-wait in growing patients
alternativeTreatments.description: In adolescents still growing, regular cephalometric monitoring and digital simulation of the anticipated adult jaw position guides the timing of intervention; no treatment is undertaken until skeletal maturity is confirmed to avoid relapse.

whySeeSpecialist: Macrogenia should be assessed by an oral and maxillofacial surgeon and an orthodontist working in a joint clinic, because the correct treatment — genioplasty alone, or combined orthognathic surgery — depends on the underlying jaw relationship as much as on the chin itself. A specialist team can distinguish isolated chin excess from a broader Class III malocclusion and can plan treatment that achieves both a balanced face and a stable occlusion. Failure to assess the occlusion before correcting the chin cosmetically can produce a worse functional outcome. Additionally, new or progressive macrogenia in an adult requires investigation for acromegaly or Paget's disease before any surgical planning.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an oral and maxillofacial surgeon with specific orthognathic surgery expertise, ideally working alongside an orthodontist with experience in orthognathic treatment. Accreditation with a specialty board (e.g., British Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons, American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons) and high procedural volume are markers of quality. Look for centres offering three-dimensional virtual surgical planning, which improves precision and predictability. If acromegaly is a possible cause, an endocrinologist should be involved in the multi-disciplinary team.
preventionStrategies: No specific prevention exists for genetically determined macrogenia; early orthodontic monitoring during adolescence allows timely treatment planning.
Early diagnosis and treatment of acromegaly prevents progressive mandibular enlargement from continuing; adults with progressive chin change should be screened for GH excess.
Treatment of Paget's disease with bisphosphonates arrests further mandibular expansion.
Deferring elective genioplasty until skeletal maturity is confirmed prevents the need for repeat surgery due to post-operative growth.
lifestyleModifications: There are no specific lifestyle interventions that reduce bony macrogenia; maintaining good overall health, a healthy weight, and managing any comorbid conditions supports surgical candidacy.
Psychological support and counselling can be valuable before and after surgical correction for individuals experiencing significant distress related to their appearance.
Following post-operative dietary restrictions (soft diet for 6-8 weeks after genioplasty) supports bone healing and reduces the risk of hardware displacement.
Maintaining excellent oral hygiene throughout combined orthodontic-surgical treatment protects dental and periodontal health during the prolonged treatment process.

recommended: Soft, easily chewable foods for 6-8 weeks after genioplasty while bone healing consolidates
Adequate protein intake to support surgical wound healing
Calcium and vitamin D-rich foods to support bone mineralisation, particularly relevant in Paget's disease management
avoid: Hard, chewy, or crunchy foods immediately after surgery that could stress healing bone or displace fixation hardware
Smoking and excess alcohol, which impair bone healing and increase surgical complication risk

exerciseGuidelines: General fitness is encouraged before surgery to optimise surgical fitness and recovery. After genioplasty, avoid contact sports and any activity with risk of facial trauma for at least six weeks while the osteotomy heals. Light cardiovascular exercise such as walking can typically resume within one to two weeks of surgery; return to full activity is guided by the surgical team based on bone healing progress confirmed on follow-up imaging.
dailyManagement: Attend all orthodontic appointments before and after surgery to maintain treatment momentum and ensure the occlusal outcome meets planned targets.
Follow post-operative wound care instructions including gentle oral rinsing with chlorhexidine and regular ice pack application in the first 48-72 hours to reduce swelling.
Take prescribed analgesia and any prophylactic antibiotics for the specified post-operative duration.

Monitor for signs of complications: progressive swelling, numbness that is worsening rather than improving, pus at the incision site, or significant pain at rest beyond the expected post-operative period.
Wear retainers as instructed after orthodontic treatment to protect the surgical result.
prognosis: The prognosis for surgically treated macrogenia is excellent when treatment is well planned and performed after skeletal maturity. Sliding genioplasty using rigid titanium plate fixation achieves long-term skeletal stability, with relapse of more than 3mm occurring in under 10 percent of cases in published series. Aesthetic satisfaction rates are high, typically 85 to 95 percent in patient-reported outcome studies. Combined orthognathic surgery for skeletal Class III relationships achieves stable functional occlusion with high satisfaction rates, though it carries greater recovery time and a longer orthodontic commitment. Macrogenia arising from acromegaly stabilises after the underlying endocrine disorder is controlled, though some permanent skeletal change may remain. Psychological outcomes are generally positive: correction of significant facial imbalance is associated with meaningful improvements in self-esteem and quality of life in most published studies.
recoveryTimeline: After isolated genioplasty, significant swelling resolves over two to three weeks; social presentability (return to work in a non-physical job) is typically achieved within ten to fourteen days. Soft diet restrictions apply for six to eight weeks while the osteotomy consolidates. Bone healing is radiographically confirmed at three to six months. Numbness of the lower lip and chin, caused by retraction of the mental nerve, is common post-operatively and resolves in the majority of patients over three to six months. After combined bimaxillary surgery, recovery is longer — two to three weeks for initial swelling, full soft tissue resolution over three to six months, and orthodontic finishing over a further six to twelve months.
complications: Inferior alveolar nerve injury producing persistent numbness or tingling of the lower lip and chin, occurring in 5-20% of genioplasty cases; the majority resolve within six months.
Skeletal relapse — gradual return toward the original chin position — occurring in under 10% of cases with rigid fixation; more common in vertical reductions than horizontal repositioning.
Wound dehiscence or infection at the intraoral incision site, typically minor and managed with topical antibiotics and mouth rinses.
Hardware complications — rare loosening or prominence of titanium mini-plates requiring removal in approximately 3-5% of patients.
Asymmetry or aesthetic dissatisfaction requiring revision surgery in a small proportion of patients, particularly when three-dimensional planning was not used.
Psychological dissatisfaction or body dysmorphic disorder presentation in patients with unrealistic expectations; careful pre-operative psychological screening is an important safeguard.

sources.title: Proffit WR, White RP, Sarver DM. Contemporary Treatment of Dentofacial Deformity. Mosby, 2003 (standard reference for orthognathic surgery classification and planning)

confusedWithConditions.name: Microgenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Microgenia is a deficiency or underdevelopment of the chin — too small or recessed — the direct opposite of macrogenia. The two are the polar ends of the spectrum of chin dimensional variation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Temporomandibular joint disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TMJ disorder produces jaw pain, clicking, and limited opening due to joint pathology; it is a consequence that can accompany jaw relationship disorders including macrogenia, not the same condition.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sleep bruxism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sleep bruxism involves teeth grinding and clenching during sleep, causing dental wear and jaw muscle pain; it may co-occur with jaw relationship disorders but does not cause or define chin prominence.

confusedWithConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Obstructive sleep apnea is a breathing disorder during sleep; it can co-occur with jaw skeletal anomalies but is a distinct condition diagnosed with polysomnography.

relatedConditions.name: Microgenia
relatedConditions.relevance: Microgenia is the opposite jaw deformity — chin deficiency — sharing the same anatomical region and surgical correction approach (genioplasty), making the two conditions closely related in surgical planning.

relatedConditions.name: Temporomandibular joint disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: TMJ dysfunction commonly co-occurs with jaw skeletal discrepancies including macrogenia and is routinely assessed before and after orthognathic surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Sleep bruxism
relatedConditions.relevance: Bruxism and jaw relationship disorders frequently co-occur; both affect the temporomandibular joint and dental occlusion and may require coordinated management.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive sleep apnea
relatedConditions.relevance: Skeletal jaw relationships including prognathism can affect upper airway anatomy; sleep apnea assessment is part of comprehensive pre-surgical evaluation.

faqs.question: What is macrogenia?
faqs.answer: Macrogenia is an over-development or excessive forward or downward projection of the chin caused by an enlarged mandibular symphysis. The chin appears disproportionately large relative to the rest of the face. It is classified under dentofacial anomalies (ICD-10: M26.05).

faqs.question: What causes a large or prominent chin?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is genetic — mandibular morphology is highly heritable, and prominent chins cluster in families. Other causes include condylar hyperplasia, acromegaly from a pituitary tumour, and Paget's disease of bone in older adults. Most cases are developmental, not caused by a medical disease.

faqs.question: How is macrogenia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses lateral cephalometric radiographs to measure chin projection against population norms, three-dimensional cone-beam CT for surgical planning, dental occlusion assessment, and standardised facial photographs. If progressive change is occurring in an adult, blood tests for acromegaly are included.

faqs.question: Can macrogenia be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: For mild cases, orthodontic camouflage — moving the lower teeth to reduce apparent chin prominence — can provide modest improvement. For clinically significant bony macrogenia, surgical genioplasty is the only reliable correction; no non-surgical procedure predictably reduces bony chin excess.

faqs.question: What is a genioplasty?
faqs.answer: Genioplasty is a surgical procedure in which the chin bone is cut and repositioned. In macrogenia, the chin is moved posteriorly to reduce forward projection, superiorly to reduce vertical height, or both, then fixed in place with titanium mini-plates. It is performed under general anaesthesia through an intraoral incision.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after genioplasty?
faqs.answer: Significant swelling resolves within 2-3 weeks. Social presentability returns in about 10-14 days. A soft diet is needed for 6-8 weeks while bone heals. Lower lip and chin numbness is common for the first few months and resolves in most patients within 3-6 months.

faqs.question: Is genioplasty permanent?
faqs.answer: Yes. Genioplasty using rigid titanium plate fixation provides durable results; relapse of more than 3mm occurs in under 10% of cases. Patients should be skeletally mature before surgery to avoid further jaw growth affecting the outcome.

faqs.question: Does macrogenia require orthognathic surgery?
faqs.answer: Not always. Isolated chin excess without dental malocclusion can be corrected with genioplasty alone. When macrogenia is part of a broader Class III skeletal jaw relationship causing malocclusion, full orthognathic surgery — repositioning both jaws — is needed for a functional and stable outcome.

faqs.question: Can macrogenia cause jaw pain or TMJ problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. When chin prominence is part of a broader jaw relationship disorder, uneven loading of the temporomandibular joints can produce pain, clicking, or limited jaw opening. TMJ assessment is a routine part of pre-surgical evaluation.

faqs.question: Can macrogenia cause sleep apnea?
faqs.answer: Macrogenia itself does not typically cause sleep apnea; however, when it is part of a broader jaw skeletal anomaly affecting the upper airway anatomy, obstructive sleep apnea may co-occur and should be screened for before treatment planning.

faqs.question: What is the difference between macrogenia and prognathism?
faqs.answer: Prognathism refers to forward projection of the entire lower jaw (mandible) relative to the upper jaw, which causes Class III dental malocclusion. Macrogenia specifically refers to an enlarged chin point (symphysis), which may occur with or without prognathism. The two often co-exist.

faqs.question: At what age can macrogenia surgery be done?
faqs.answer: Surgery is deferred until skeletal maturity: approximately 16-17 years in females and 18-19 years in males, confirmed by stable serial cephalometric measurements over at least 12 months. Operating before maturity risks relapse as the jaw continues to grow.

faqs.question: Is macrogenia the same as a double chin?
faqs.answer: No. A double chin refers to submental fat accumulation under the jaw, which is related to body weight and skin laxity. Macrogenia is a bony deformity — an excessively large or protruding chin bone. They are distinct conditions requiring different treatment.

faqs.question: Does macrogenia run in families?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mandibular morphology, including chin projection, is among the more heritable facial characteristics. Twin studies estimate heritability of chin position at 60 to 80 percent. Prominent chins and Class III jaw relationships frequently cluster across generations in families.

faqs.question: Can a prominent chin be caused by a hormone condition?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acromegaly — growth hormone excess from a pituitary tumour — causes progressive bony enlargement of the mandible and chin in adults. Any adult with a progressively growing chin should have serum IGF-1 measured to screen for this treatable condition.

faqs.question: What risks are associated with genioplasty?
faqs.answer: The main risks are temporary or persistent numbness of the lower lip and chin (from mental nerve retraction, occurring in 5-20% of patients), minor wound infection, hardware loosening requiring plate removal (3-5%), minor relapse, and rarely asymmetry requiring revision surgery.

faqs.question: How long does genioplasty surgery take?
faqs.answer: Isolated genioplasty typically takes 1 to 2 hours under general anaesthesia. It is usually performed as a day-case or short-stay procedure. Combined orthognathic surgery including both jaws takes 2 to 5 hours and requires an overnight hospital stay.

faqs.question: What is virtual surgical planning for jaw surgery?
faqs.answer: Virtual surgical planning uses cone-beam CT data and software to simulate the surgery in three dimensions before operating. The planned movements guide the fabrication of custom surgical guides and splints, improving precision and predictability of the result compared with traditional planning methods.

faqs.question: Is macrogenia the same as acromegaly?
faqs.answer: No. Macrogenia is a structural jaw deformity; acromegaly is an endocrine disease caused by excess growth hormone that can produce macrogenia as one of its manifestations. Most macrogenia is genetic or developmental, not caused by acromegaly.

faqs.question: What should I do if I am concerned about a prominent chin?
faqs.answer: See an orthodontist or oral and maxillofacial surgeon for an initial assessment. They will take clinical measurements, a lateral cephalometric X-ray, and dental impressions to determine whether the prominence is bony or soft tissue, whether there is an underlying jaw relationship issue, and what treatment options are most appropriate.

metaDescription: Macrogenia is an oversized or protruding chin from mandibular bone excess. Learn causes, cephalometric diagnosis, genioplasty and orthognathic surgery options.
